**Auckland**[[a]](#cite_note-5) is a large metropolitan city in the [North Island](/wiki/North_Island "North Island") of [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"). It has an urban population of about 1,547,200 (June 2025).[[2]](#cite_note-NZ_population_data_2018-2) It is located in the greater [Auckland Region](/wiki/Auckland_Region "Auckland Region"), the area governed by [Auckland Council](/wiki/Auckland_Council "Auckland Council"), which includes outlying rural areas and the islands of the [Hauraki Gulf](/wiki/Hauraki_Gulf "Hauraki Gulf"), and which has a total population of 1,816,000 as of June 2025.[[2]](#cite_note-NZ_population_data_2018-2) It is the [most populous city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_New_Zealand "List of cities in New Zealand") of New Zealand and the [fifth-largest city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Oceania_by_population "List of cities in Oceania by population") in [Oceania](/wiki/Oceania "Oceania").

The city lies between the [Hauraki Gulf](/wiki/Hauraki_Gulf "Hauraki Gulf") to the east, the [Hunua Ranges](/wiki/Hunua_Ranges "Hunua Ranges") to the south-east, the [Manukau Harbour](/wiki/Manukau_Harbour "Manukau Harbour") to the south-west, and the [Waitākere Ranges](/wiki/Wait%C4%81kere_Ranges "Waitākere Ranges") and smaller ranges to the west and north-west. The surrounding hills are covered in [rainforest](/wiki/Rainforest "Rainforest") and the landscape is dotted with 53 volcanic centres that make up the [Auckland Volcanic Field](/wiki/Auckland_Volcanic_Field "Auckland Volcanic Field"). The central part of the urban area occupies a narrow [isthmus](/wiki/Isthmus "Isthmus") between the Manukau Harbour on the [Tasman Sea](/wiki/Tasman_Sea "Tasman Sea") and the [Waitematā Harbour](/wiki/Waitemat%C4%81_Harbour "Waitematā Harbour") on the Pacific Ocean. Auckland is one of the few cities in the world to have a harbour on each of two separate major bodies of water.

The [Auckland isthmus](/wiki/Auckland_isthmus "Auckland isthmus") was first settled c. 1350 and was valued for its rich and fertile land. The [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people") population in the area is estimated to have peaked at 20,000 before the arrival of Europeans.[[5]](#cite_note-Hochstetter18672-6) After a [British colony](/wiki/Colony_of_New_Zealand "Colony of New Zealand") was established in New Zealand in 1840, [William Hobson](/wiki/William_Hobson "William Hobson"), then Lieutenant-Governor of New Zealand, chose Auckland as its new [capital](/wiki/Capital_of_New_Zealand "Capital of New Zealand"). [Ngāti Whātua Ōrākei](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Wh%C4%81tua_%C5%8Cr%C4%81kei "Ngāti Whātua Ōrākei") made a strategic gift of land to Hobson for the new capital. Māori–European conflict over land in the region led to war in the mid-19th century. In 1865, Auckland was replaced by [Wellington](/wiki/Wellington "Wellington") as the capital, but continued to grow, initially because of its port and the logging and gold-mining activities in its hinterland, and later because of pastoral farming (especially dairy farming) in the surrounding area, and manufacturing in the city itself.[[6]](#cite_note-7) It has been the nation's largest city throughout most of its history. Today, [Auckland's central business district](/wiki/Auckland_CBD "Auckland CBD") is New Zealand's leading economic hub.

While [Europeans](/wiki/European_New_Zealanders "European New Zealanders") continue to make up the plurality of Auckland's population, the city became multicultural and [cosmopolitan](/wiki/Cosmopolitanism "Cosmopolitanism") in the late 20th century, with [Asians](/wiki/Asian_New_Zealanders "Asian New Zealanders") accounting for 34.9% of the city's population in 2023.[[7]](#cite_note-Census_2023-8) In 2016 Auckland had the fourth largest [foreign-born](/wiki/Foreign_born "Foreign born") population in the world, with 39% of its residents born overseas.[[8]](#cite_note-9) With its sizeable population of [Pasifika New Zealanders](/wiki/Pasifika_New_Zealanders "Pasifika New Zealanders"), the city is also home to the largest ethnic [Polynesian](/wiki/Polynesians "Polynesians") population in the world.[[9]](#cite_note-AAAROUND-10)

The [University of Auckland](/wiki/University_of_Auckland "University of Auckland"), founded in 1883, is the largest university in New Zealand. The city's significant tourist attractions include national historic sites, festivals, performing arts, sports activities and a variety of cultural institutions, such as the [Auckland War Memorial Museum](/wiki/Auckland_War_Memorial_Museum "Auckland War Memorial Museum"), the [Museum of Transport and Technology](/wiki/Museum_of_Transport_and_Technology "Museum of Transport and Technology"), and the [Auckland Art Gallery Toi o Tāmaki](/wiki/Auckland_Art_Gallery_Toi_o_T%C4%81maki "Auckland Art Gallery Toi o Tāmaki"). Its architectural landmarks include the [Harbour Bridge](/wiki/Auckland_Harbour_Bridge "Auckland Harbour Bridge"), the [Town Hall](/wiki/Auckland_Town_Hall "Auckland Town Hall"), the [Ferry Building](/wiki/Auckland_Ferry_Terminal "Auckland Ferry Terminal") and the [Sky Tower](/wiki/Sky_Tower_(Auckland) "Sky Tower (Auckland)"), which is the second-tallest building in the [Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere") after [Thamrin Nine](/wiki/Thamrin_Nine "Thamrin Nine").[[10]](#cite_note-11) The city is served by [Auckland Airport](/wiki/Auckland_Airport "Auckland Airport"), which handled 18.5 million passengers in 2024.[[11]](#cite_note-12) Auckland is one of the world's [most liveable cities](/wiki/Most_liveable_cities "Most liveable cities"), ranking fifth in the 2024 Mercer Quality of Living Survey and ninth in the 2024 [Global Liveability Ranking](/wiki/Global_Liveability_Ranking "Global Liveability Ranking") by *[The Economist](/wiki/The_Economist "The Economist")*.[[12]](#cite_note-13)[[13]](#cite_note-14)

## Toponymy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auckland&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Toponymy")]

The [Māori-language](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language "Māori language") name for Auckland is **Tāmaki Makaurau**, meaning "Tāmaki desired by many", in reference to the desirability of its natural resources and geography.[[14]](#cite_note-15) There are various theories of the origin of the name "[Tāmaki](/wiki/T%C4%81maki "Tāmaki")", which is also used to refer to an eastern suburb of Auckland. It is regarded by some to be the isthmus between the two harbours of the area, which is variously said to be named after a son of Maruiwi from [Taranaki](/wiki/Taranaki "Taranaki"), a line of chiefs from the southern Taranaki, or a female leader of [Ngāti Te Ata](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Te_Ata "Ngāti Te Ata"). Other versions of the name include *Tāmakinui* (great Tāmaki) or *Tāmaki-herehere-ngā-waka* (Tāmaki that binds many canoes).[[15]](#cite_note-16)[[16]](#cite_note-17)

[William Hobson](/wiki/William_Hobson "William Hobson") named the area after [George Eden, Earl of Auckland](/wiki/George_Eden,_Earl_of_Auckland "George Eden, Earl of Auckland"), British [First Lord of the Admiralty](/wiki/First_Lord_of_the_Admiralty "First Lord of the Admiralty"). The Earldom of Auckland was named after [West Auckland](/wiki/West_Auckland,_County_Durham "West Auckland, County Durham"), a village in [County Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham"), [Northern England](/wiki/Northern_England "Northern England").[[17]](#cite_note-18) The name "Auckland" in West Auckland is thought to originate from the [Cumbric](/wiki/Cumbric_language "Cumbric language") word "*Alclud*", which was the [Kingdom of Strathclyde](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Strathclyde "Kingdom of Strathclyde")'s alternative name meaning "cliff on the Clyde". It is thought 'Clyde' may be the [river Gaunless](/wiki/River_Gaunless "River Gaunless")' old name.[[18]](#cite_note-Gelling-19)[[19]](#cite_note-Oxford-20)[[20]](#cite_note-heroicage.org-21)

Auckland is popularly nicknamed the "City of Sails" or the "Queen City".[[21]](#cite_note-City_of_Sails-22)[[22]](#cite_note-23)

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Auckland](/wiki/Timeline_of_Auckland "Timeline of Auckland").

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auckland&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/One_Tree_Hill%2C_Auckland%2C_March_2015.jpg/250px-One_Tree_Hill%2C_Auckland%2C_March_2015.jpg)](/wiki/File:One_Tree_Hill,_Auckland,_March_2015.jpg)

[Maungakiekie / One Tree Hill](/wiki/Maungakiekie_/_One_Tree_Hill "Maungakiekie / One Tree Hill"), with Auckland city in the background, showing terraces of the Māori pā[[23]](#cite_note-24)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Auckland%2C_1853_SLNSW_FL3270469.jpg/250px-Auckland%2C_1853_SLNSW_FL3270469.jpg)](/wiki/File:Auckland,_1853_SLNSW_FL3270469.jpg)

Auckland, 1853

The [Auckland isthmus](/wiki/Auckland_isthmus "Auckland isthmus") was settled by [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people") around 1350, and was valued for its rich and fertile land. Many [pā](/wiki/P%C4%81_(M%C4%81ori) "Pā (Māori)") (fortified villages) were built, mainly on the volcanic peaks. By the early 1700s, [Te Waiohua](/wiki/Te_Waiohua "Te Waiohua"), a confederation of tribes such as [Ngā Oho](/wiki/Ng%C4%81_Oho "Ngā Oho"), Ngā Riki and Ngā Iwi, was the main tribal group on the Auckland isthmus,[[24]](#cite_note-Rawhiri-25)[[25]](#cite_note-AucklandCouncil-26) with major pā at [Maungakiekie / One Tree Hill](/wiki/Maungakiekie_/_One_Tree_Hill "Maungakiekie / One Tree Hill"), [Māngere Mountain](/wiki/M%C4%81ngere_Mountain "Māngere Mountain") and [Maungataketake](/wiki/Maungataketake "Maungataketake").[[26]](#cite_note-MJ_0015-27) The confederation came to an end around 1741 when [paramount chief](/wiki/Paramount_chief "Paramount chief") [Kiwi Tāmaki](/wiki/Kiwi_T%C4%81maki "Kiwi Tāmaki") was killed in battle by Te Waha-akiaki, a chief of the [Ngāti Whātua](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Wh%C4%81tua "Ngāti Whātua") [hapū](/wiki/Hap%C5%AB "Hapū") [Te Taoū](/wiki/Te_Tao%C5%AB "Te Taoū").[[27]](#cite_note-AileenFox-28) From the 1740s, [Ngāti Whātua Ōrākei](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Wh%C4%81tua-o-%C5%8Cr%C4%81kei "Ngāti Whātua-o-Ōrākei") were the main tribe on the Auckland isthmus.[[24]](#cite_note-Rawhiri-25) The Māori population in the area is estimated to have been about 20,000 before the arrival of Europeans.[[28]](#cite_note-Hochstetter1867-29)[[29]](#cite_note-30) The introduction of firearms at the end of the eighteenth century, which began in [Northland](/wiki/Northland_region "Northland region"), upset the balance of power and led to devastating [intertribal warfare](/wiki/Musket_Wars "Musket Wars") beginning in 1807, causing [iwi](/wiki/Iwi "Iwi") who lacked the new weapons to seek refuge in areas less exposed to coastal raids. As a result, the region had relatively low numbers of Māori when settlement by [European New Zealanders](/wiki/European_New_Zealanders "European New Zealanders") began.[[30]](#cite_note-31)[[31]](#cite_note-32)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/HURSTHOUSE%281857%29_p225_AUCKLAND%2C_NEW_ZEALAND.jpg/250px-HURSTHOUSE%281857%29_p225_AUCKLAND%2C_NEW_ZEALAND.jpg)](/wiki/File:HURSTHOUSE(1857)_p225_AUCKLAND,_NEW_ZEALAND.jpg)

Print of a painting of Auckland port, 1857

On 20 March 1840 in the [Manukau Harbour](/wiki/Manukau_Harbour "Manukau Harbour") area where Ngāti Whātua farmed, paramount chief [Apihai Te Kawau](/wiki/Apihai_Te_Kawau "Apihai Te Kawau") signed the [Treaty of Waitangi](/wiki/Treaty_of_Waitangi "Treaty of Waitangi").[[32]](#cite_note-33) Ngāti Whātua sought British protection from [Ngāpuhi](/wiki/Ng%C4%81puhi "Ngāpuhi") as well as a reciprocal relationship with the [Crown](/wiki/Monarchy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Monarchy of the United Kingdom") and the [Church](/wiki/Christian_Church "Christian Church"). Soon after signing the treaty, Ngāti Whātua Ōrākei made a strategic gift of 3,500 acres (1,400 ha) of land on the [Waitematā Harbour](/wiki/Waitemat%C4%81_Harbour "Waitematā Harbour") to the new Governor of New Zealand, [William Hobson](/wiki/William_Hobson "William Hobson"), for the new [capital](/wiki/Capital_of_New_Zealand "Capital of New Zealand"), which Hobson named for [George Eden, Earl of Auckland](/wiki/George_Eden,_1st_Earl_of_Auckland "George Eden, 1st Earl of Auckland"), then [Viceroy of India](/wiki/Viceroy_of_India "Viceroy of India").[[33]](#cite_note-Apihai-34)[[34]](#cite_note-Values-35)[[35]](#cite_note-36) Auckland was founded on 18 September 1840 and was officially declared New Zealand's capital in 1841,[[36]](#cite_note-37)[[37]](#cite_note-38) and the transfer of the administration from Russell (now [Old Russell](/wiki/Okiato "Okiato")) in the Bay of Islands was completed in 1842. However, even in 1840 [Port Nicholson](/wiki/Port_Nicholson "Port Nicholson") (later renamed [Wellington](/wiki/Wellington "Wellington")) was seen as a better choice for an administrative capital because of its proximity to the [South Island](/wiki/South_Island "South Island"), and Wellington became the capital in 1865. After losing its status as capital, Auckland remained the principal city of the [Auckland Province](/wiki/Auckland_Province "Auckland Province") until the provincial system was abolished in 1876.[[38]](#cite_note-39)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Mid_Queen_Street.jpg/250px-Mid_Queen_Street.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mid_Queen_Street.jpg)

Queen Street (c. 1889); painting by [Jacques Carabain](/wiki/Jacques_Carabain "Jacques Carabain"). Most of the buildings depicted were demolished in the 1970s.[[39]](#cite_note-40)

In response to the ongoing rebellion by [Hōne Heke](/wiki/H%C5%8Dne_Heke "Hōne Heke") in the mid-1840s, the government encouraged retired but fit British soldiers and their families to migrate to Auckland to form a defence line around the port settlement as garrison soldiers. By the time the first [Fencibles](/wiki/Fencibles "Fencibles") arrived in 1848, the [Northern War](/wiki/Flagstaff_War "Flagstaff War") had concluded. Outlying defensive towns were then constructed to the south, stretching in a line from the port village of [Onehunga](/wiki/Onehunga "Onehunga") in the west to [Howick](/wiki/Howick,_New_Zealand "Howick, New Zealand") in the east. Each of the four settlements had about 800 settlers.[[40]](#cite_note-:2-41)

In the early 1860s, Auckland became a base against the [Māori King Movement](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_King_Movement "Māori King Movement"),[[41]](#cite_note-42) and the 12,000 Imperial soldiers stationed there gave a strong boost to local commerce.[[42]](#cite_note-Essay_Vincent-43) This, and [continued road building towards the south](/wiki/Great_South_Road,_New_Zealand "Great South Road, New Zealand") into the [Waikato](/wiki/Waikato "Waikato") region, enabled [Pākehā](/wiki/P%C4%81keh%C4%81 "Pākehā") (European New Zealanders) influence to spread from Auckland. The city's population grew fairly rapidly, from 1,500 in 1841 to 3,635 in 1845,[[42]](#cite_note-Essay_Vincent-43) then to 12,423 by 1864. The growth occurred similarly to other [mercantile](/wiki/Commerce "Commerce")-dominated cities, mainly around the port and with problems of overcrowding and pollution. Auckland's population of ex-soldiers was far greater than that of other settlements: about 50 per cent of the population was Irish, which contrasted heavily with the majority English settlers in Wellington, [Christchurch](/wiki/Christchurch "Christchurch") or [New Plymouth](/wiki/New_Plymouth "New Plymouth"). The majority of settlers in the early period were assisted by receiving cheap passage to New Zealand.[[43]](#cite_note-44)

### Modern history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auckland&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Modern history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Auckland_CBD_And_Waterfront_In_The_1950s.jpg/250px-Auckland_CBD_And_Waterfront_In_The_1950s.jpg)](/wiki/File:Auckland_CBD_And_Waterfront_In_The_1950s.jpg)

Looking east over the area that became [Wynyard Quarter](/wiki/Wynyard_Quarter "Wynyard Quarter") with the [Auckland CBD](/wiki/Auckland_CBD "Auckland CBD") in the middle distance, c. 1950s

Trams and railway lines shaped Auckland's rapid expansion in the early first half of the 20th century. However, after the Second World War, the city's transport system and urban form became increasingly dominated by the motor vehicle.[[40]](#cite_note-:2-41) Arterial roads and motorways became both defining and geographically dividing features of the urban landscape. They also allowed further massive expansion that resulted in the growth of suburban areas such as the [North Shore](/wiki/North_Shore,_New_Zealand "North Shore, New Zealand") (especially after the construction of the [Auckland Harbour Bridge](/wiki/Auckland_Harbour_Bridge "Auckland Harbour Bridge") in the late 1950s), and [Manukau City](/wiki/Manukau_City "Manukau City") in the south.[[44]](#cite_note-45)

Economic deregulation in the mid-1980s led to very dramatic changes to Auckland's economy, and many companies relocated their head offices from Wellington to Auckland. The region was now the nerve centre of the entire national economy. Auckland also benefited from a surge in tourism, which brought 75 per cent of New Zealand's international visitors through its airport. Auckland's port handled 31 per cent of the country's container trade in 2015.[[45]](#cite_note-teara.govt.nz-46)

The face of urban Auckland changed when the government's immigration policy began allowing immigrants from Asia in 1986. This has led to Auckland becoming a multicultural city, with people of all ethnic backgrounds. According to the 1961 census data, Māori and Pacific Islanders made up five per cent of Auckland's population; Asians less than one per cent.[[46]](#cite_note-47) The city became home to the world's largest Polynesian population by the 1990s.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] By 2006, the Asian population had reached 18.0 per cent in Auckland, and 36.2 per cent in the central city. New arrivals from Hong Kong, [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") and [Korea](/wiki/Korea "Korea") gave a distinctive character to the areas where they clustered, while a range of other immigrants introduced mosques, [Hindu](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") temples, [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") temples, [halal](/wiki/Halal "Halal") and [kosher](/wiki/Kosher "Kosher") butchers and ethnic restaurants to the suburbs.[[45]](#cite_note-teara.govt.nz-46)

### Scope

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auckland&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Scope")]

Further information: [List of suburbs of Auckland](/wiki/List_of_suburbs_of_Auckland "List of suburbs of Auckland"), [South Auckland](/wiki/South_Auckland "South Auckland"), [East Auckland](/wiki/East_Auckland "East Auckland"), [West Auckland](/wiki/West_Auckland,_New_Zealand "West Auckland, New Zealand"), and [North Shore](/wiki/North_Shore,_New_Zealand "North Shore, New Zealand")

The boundaries of Auckland are imprecisely defined. The Auckland [urban area](/wiki/Urban_areas_of_New_Zealand "Urban areas of New Zealand"), as it is defined by [Statistics New Zealand](/wiki/Statistics_New_Zealand "Statistics New Zealand") under the *Statistical Standard for Geographic Areas 2018* (SSGA18), spans 607.07 square kilometres (234.39 sq mi) and extends to [Long Bay](/wiki/Long_Bay,_New_Zealand "Long Bay, New Zealand") in the north, [Swanson](/wiki/Swanson,_New_Zealand "Swanson, New Zealand") in the north-west, and Runciman in the south.[[1]](#cite_note-Area-1) Auckland's [functional urban area](/wiki/Larger_urban_zone "Larger urban zone") (commuting zone) extends from just south of [Warkworth](/wiki/Warkworth,_New_Zealand "Warkworth, New Zealand") in the north to [Meremere](/wiki/Meremere "Meremere") in the south, incorporating the [Hibiscus Coast](/wiki/Hibiscus_Coast "Hibiscus Coast") in the northeast, [Helensville](/wiki/Helensville "Helensville"), [Parakai](/wiki/Parakai "Parakai"), [Muriwai](/wiki/Muriwai "Muriwai"), [Waimauku](/wiki/Waimauku "Waimauku"), [Kumeū](/wiki/Kume%C5%AB "Kumeū")-[Huapai](/wiki/Huapai "Huapai"), and [Riverhead](/wiki/Riverhead,_New_Zealand "Riverhead, New Zealand") in the northwest, [Beachlands-Pine Harbour](/wiki/Beachlands,_New_Zealand "Beachlands, New Zealand") and [Maraetai](/wiki/Maraetai "Maraetai") in the east, and [Pukekohe](/wiki/Pukekohe "Pukekohe"), [Clarks Beach](/wiki/Clarks_Beach "Clarks Beach"), [Patumāhoe](/wiki/Patumahoe "Patumahoe"), [Waiuku](/wiki/Waiuku "Waiuku"), [Tuakau](/wiki/Tuakau "Tuakau") and [Pōkeno](/wiki/P%C5%8Dkeno "Pōkeno") (the latter two in the Waikato region) in the south.[[47]](#cite_note-48) Auckland forms [New Zealand's largest urban area](/wiki/List_of_New_Zealand_urban_areas_by_population "List of New Zealand urban areas by population").[[2]](#cite_note-NZ_population_data_2018-2)

The Auckland urban area lies within the [Auckland Region](/wiki/Auckland_Region "Auckland Region"), an administrative region that takes its name from the city. The region encompasses the city centre, as well as suburbs, surrounding towns, nearshore islands, and rural areas north and south of the urban area.[[48]](#cite_note-49)

The [Auckland central business district](/wiki/Auckland_central_business_district "Auckland central business district") is the most built-up area of the region. The CBD covers 433 hectares (1,070 acres) in a triangular area,[[49]](#cite_note-CBD-50) and is bounded by the [Auckland waterfront](/wiki/Auckland_waterfront "Auckland waterfront") on the Waitematā Harbour[[50]](#cite_note-51) and the inner-city suburbs of [Ponsonby](/wiki/Ponsonby,_New_Zealand "Ponsonby, New Zealand"), [Newton](/wiki/Newton,_New_Zealand "Newton, New Zealand") and [Parnell](/wiki/Parnell,_New_Zealand "Parnell, New Zealand").[[49]](#cite_note-CBD-50)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/AucklandPano_MC.jpg/1280px-AucklandPano_MC.jpg)](/wiki/File:AucklandPano_MC.jpg "Auckland cityscape viewed from Maungawhau / Mount Eden. The nearer body of water is the Waitematā Harbour and the further one is the Hauraki Gulf.")

Auckland cityscape viewed from Maungawhau / Mount Eden. The nearer body of water is the [Waitematā Harbour](/wiki/Waitemat%C4%81_Harbour "Waitematā Harbour") and the further one is the [Hauraki Gulf](/wiki/Hauraki_Gulf "Hauraki Gulf").

### Harbours and gulf

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auckland&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Harbours and gulf")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Auckland_isthmus_and_Waitemata_Harbour.jpg/250px-Auckland_isthmus_and_Waitemata_Harbour.jpg)](/wiki/File:Auckland_isthmus_and_Waitemata_Harbour.jpg)

Satellite view of the [Auckland isthmus](/wiki/Auckland_isthmus "Auckland isthmus") with Manukau (lower) and Waitematā (upper) Harbours

The central areas of the city are located on the [Auckland isthmus](/wiki/Auckland_isthmus "Auckland isthmus"), less than two kilometres wide at its narrowest point, between [Māngere Inlet](/wiki/M%C4%81ngere_Inlet "Māngere Inlet") and the [Tāmaki River](/wiki/T%C4%81maki_River "Tāmaki River"). There are two harbours surrounding this isthmus: [Waitematā Harbour](/wiki/Waitemat%C4%81_Harbour "Waitematā Harbour") to the north, which extends east to the [Hauraki Gulf](/wiki/Hauraki_Gulf "Hauraki Gulf") and thence to the Pacific Ocean, and [Manukau Harbour](/wiki/Manukau_Harbour "Manukau Harbour") to the south, which opens west to the [Tasman Sea](/wiki/Tasman_Sea "Tasman Sea").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Bridges span parts of both harbours, notably the [Auckland Harbour Bridge](/wiki/Auckland_Harbour_Bridge "Auckland Harbour Bridge") crossing the Waitematā Harbour west of the central business district. The [Māngere Bridge](/wiki/M%C4%81ngere_Bridge_(bridges) "Māngere Bridge (bridges)") and the [Upper Harbour Bridge](/wiki/Upper_Harbour_Bridge "Upper Harbour Bridge") span the upper reaches of the Manukau and Waitematā Harbours, respectively. In earlier times, [portages](/wiki/Portages_of_New_Zealand "Portages of New Zealand") crossed the narrowest sections of the isthmus.[[51]](#cite_note-Furey-52)[[52]](#cite_note-TeKawerau-53)

Several islands of the Hauraki Gulf are administered as part of the Auckland Region, though they are not part of the Auckland urban area. Parts of [Waiheke Island](/wiki/Waiheke_Island "Waiheke Island") effectively function as Auckland suburbs, while various smaller islands near Auckland are mostly zoned 'recreational open space' or are nature sanctuaries.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auckland&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Climate")]

Under the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"), Auckland has an [oceanic climate](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate") (Köppen climate classification *Cfb*). However, under the [Trewartha climate classification](/wiki/Trewartha_climate_classification "Trewartha climate classification") and according to the [National Institute of Water and Atmospheric Research](/wiki/National_Institute_of_Water_and_Atmospheric_Research "National Institute of Water and Atmospheric Research") (NIWA), the city's climate is classified as [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") with warm summers and mild winters (Trewartha climate classification *Cfbl*).[[53]](#cite_note-Peel-54)[[54]](#cite_note-55) It is the warmest main centre of New Zealand.

[Snowfall](/wiki/Snowfall "Snowfall") is extremely rare: the most significant fall since the start of the 20th century was on 27 July 1939, when snow fell just before dawn and five centimetres (2 in) of snow reportedly lay on [Mount Eden](/wiki/Maungawhau "Maungawhau").[[55]](#cite_note-snow1-56)[[56]](#cite_note-57) Snowflakes were also seen on 28 July 1930 and 15 August 2011.[[57]](#cite_note-58)[[58]](#cite_note-snow2-59)[[59]](#cite_note-60)

[Frosts](/wiki/Frost "Frost") in Auckland are infrequent and often localised. Henderson Riverpark receives an annual average of 27.4 [ground frosts](/wiki/Ground_frost "Ground frost") per year, while Auckland Airport receives an annual average of 8.7 ground frosts per year.[[60]](#cite_note-:1-61)

Average [sea temperature](/wiki/Sea_surface_temperature "Sea surface temperature") around Auckland varies throughout the year. The water temperature is warmest in February when it averages 21 °C (70 °F), while in August, the water temperature is at its coolest, averaging 14 °C (57 °F).[[61]](#cite_note-62)

[Prevailing winds](/wiki/Prevailing_winds "Prevailing winds") in Auckland are predominantly from the southwest. The mean annual wind speed for Auckland Airport is 18 kilometres per hour (11 mph).[[62]](#cite_note-63) During the summer months there is often a [sea breeze](/wiki/Sea_breeze "Sea breeze") in Auckland which starts in the morning and dies down again in the evening.[[63]](#cite_note-64) The early morning calm on the isthmus during settled weather, before the sea breeze rises, was described as early as 1853: "In all seasons, the beauty of the day is in the early morning. At that time, generally, a solemn stillness holds, and a perfect calm prevails...".[[64]](#cite_note-1853_morning-65)

[Fog](/wiki/Fog "Fog") is a common occurrence for Auckland, especially in autumn and winter. Whenuapai Airport experiences an average of 44 fog days per year.[[65]](#cite_note-66)

Auckland occasionally suffers from air pollution due to [fine particle](/wiki/Atmospheric_particulate_matter "Atmospheric particulate matter") emissions.[[66]](#cite_note-67) There are also occasional breaches of guideline levels of [carbon monoxide](/wiki/Carbon_monoxide "Carbon monoxide").[[67]](#cite_note-68) While maritime winds normally disperse the pollution relatively quickly it can sometimes become visible as smog, especially on calm winter days.[[68]](#cite_note-69)

The record low temperature is −5.7 °C (21.7 °F) recorded at [Riverhead Forest](/wiki/Riverhead_Forest "Riverhead Forest") in June 1936, while the maximum recorded temperature is 34.0 °C (93.2 °F) on 12 February 2009.[[60]](#cite_note-:1-61) The record lowest daily maximum is 8.0 °C (46.4 °F) recorded on both 13 June 1976 and 16 August 2011.[[69]](#cite_note-Auckland_Aero_CLIDB-70) The record highest daily minimum is 23.6 °C (74.5 °F) on 12 February 2022.[[69]](#cite_note-Auckland_Aero_CLIDB-70) The record highest dew point is 25.0 °C (77.0 °F) recorded on 10 March 1972.[[69]](#cite_note-Auckland_Aero_CLIDB-70) The most humid month on record was January 2018 with an average dew point of 18.0 °C (64.4 °F).[[69]](#cite_note-Auckland_Aero_CLIDB-70) The warmest month on record was February 1990 with an average mean temperature of 22.1 °C (71.8 °F).[[69]](#cite_note-Auckland_Aero_CLIDB-70)

### Volcanoes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auckland&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Volcanoes")]

Further information: [Auckland volcanic field](/wiki/Auckland_volcanic_field "Auckland volcanic field")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Rangitoto_Island_as_seen_from_Mount_Victoria_Reserve_in_Devonport%2C_North_Shore_City_20100128_1.jpg/330px-Rangitoto_Island_as_seen_from_Mount_Victoria_Reserve_in_Devonport%2C_North_Shore_City_20100128_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rangitoto_Island_as_seen_from_Mount_Victoria_Reserve_in_Devonport,_North_Shore_City_20100128_1.jpg)

The volcanic [Rangitoto Island](/wiki/Rangitoto_Island "Rangitoto Island") in the Hauraki Gulf, with the remnant of [Takaroro / Mount Cambria](/wiki/Mount_Cambria "Mount Cambria") in the foreground (yellow, grassy reserve). Viewed from [Takarunga / Mount Victoria](/wiki/Mount_Victoria_(Auckland) "Mount Victoria (Auckland)") over [Devonport](/wiki/Devonport,_New_Zealand "Devonport, New Zealand").

The city of Auckland straddles the [Auckland Volcanic Field](/wiki/Auckland_Volcanic_Field "Auckland Volcanic Field"), an area which in the past, produced at least 53 small [volcanic](/wiki/Volcano "Volcano") centres over the last ~193,000 years, represented by a range of surface features including [maars](/wiki/Maars "Maars") (explosion craters), [tuff rings](/wiki/Tuff_ring "Tuff ring"), [scoria](/wiki/Scoria "Scoria") cones, and [lava](/wiki/Lava "Lava") flows.[[81]](#cite_note-Hopkins_et_al_(2020)-82)[[82]](#cite_note-Hayward_(2019)-83) It is fed entirely by [basaltic](/wiki/Basaltic "Basaltic") [magma](/wiki/Magma "Magma") sourced from the [mantle](/wiki/Earth%27s_mantle "Earth's mantle") at a depth of 70–90 km below the city,[[81]](#cite_note-Hopkins_et_al_(2020)-82) and is unrelated to the explosive, [subduction](/wiki/Subduction "Subduction")-driven volcanism of the [Taupō Volcanic Zone](/wiki/Taup%C5%8D_Volcanic_Zone "Taupō Volcanic Zone") in the Central North Island region of Aotearoa, New Zealand, ~250 km away. The Auckland Volcanic Field is considered to be a [monogenetic volcanic field](/wiki/Monogenetic_volcanic_field "Monogenetic volcanic field"), with each volcano erupting only a single time, usually over a timeframe of weeks to years before cessation of activity.[[82]](#cite_note-Hayward_(2019)-83) Future eruptive activity remains a threat to the city, and will likely occur at a new, unknown location within the field.[[81]](#cite_note-Hopkins_et_al_(2020)-82) The most recent activity occurred approximately 1450 AD at the [Rangitoto Volcano](/wiki/Rangitoto_Island "Rangitoto Island").[[81]](#cite_note-Hopkins_et_al_(2020)-82) This event was witnessed by [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people") occupants of the area, making it the only eruption within the Auckland Volcanic Field thus far to have been observed by humans.

The Auckland Volcanic Field has contributed greatly to the growth and prosperity of the Auckland Region since the area was settled by humans. Initially, the [maunga](/wiki/Maunga "Maunga") (scoria cones) were occupied and established as [pā](/wiki/P%C4%81 "Pā") (fortified settlements) by Māori due to the strategic advantage their elevation provided in controlling resources and key [portages](/wiki/Portages "Portages") between the [Waitematā](/wiki/Waitemat%C4%81_Harbour "Waitematā Harbour") and [Manukau](/wiki/Manukau_Harbour "Manukau Harbour") harbours.[[82]](#cite_note-Hayward_(2019)-83) The rich volcanic soils found in these areas also proved ideal for the cultivation of crops, such as [kūmara](/wiki/Sweet_potato#Oceania "Sweet potato"). Following European arrival, many of the maunga were transformed into quarries to supply the growing city with aggregate and building materials, and as a result were severely damaged or entirely destroyed.[[82]](#cite_note-Hayward_(2019)-83) A number of the smaller maar craters and tuff rings were also removed during earthworks. Most of the remaining volcanic centres are now preserved within recreational reserves administered by [Auckland Council](/wiki/Auckland_Council "Auckland Council"), the [Department of Conservation](/wiki/Department_of_Conservation_(New_Zealand) "Department of Conservation (New Zealand)"), and the [Tūpuna Maunga o Tāmaki Makaurau Authority](/wiki/T%C5%ABpuna_Maunga_o_T%C4%81maki_Makaurau "Tūpuna Maunga o Tāmaki Makaurau").

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auckland&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Demographics")]

Main article: [Demographics of Auckland](/wiki/Demographics_of_Auckland "Demographics of Auckland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Auckland_population_pyramid_in_2022.svg/250px-Auckland_population_pyramid_in_2022.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Auckland_population_pyramid_in_2022.svg)

Auckland population pyramid in 2022

The Auckland urban area, as defined by Statistics New Zealand, covers 605.67 km2 (233.85 sq mi).[[1]](#cite_note-Area-1) The urban area has an estimated population of 1,547,200 as of June 2025, 29.1 percent of [New Zealand's population](/wiki/Demographics_of_New_Zealand "Demographics of New Zealand"). The city has a population larger than the entire [South Island](/wiki/South_Island "South Island") (1,256,700).[[2]](#cite_note-NZ_population_data_2018-2)

The urban area had a population of 1,402,275 in the [2023 New Zealand census](/wiki/2023_New_Zealand_census "2023 New Zealand census"), an increase of 56,442 people (4.2%) since the [2018 census](/wiki/2018_New_Zealand_census "2018 New Zealand census"), and an increase of 178,734 people (14.6%) since the [2013 census](/wiki/2013_New_Zealand_census "2013 New Zealand census"). There were 692,490 males, 704,607 females and 5,178 people of [other genders](/wiki/Non-binary_gender "Non-binary gender") in 454,239 dwellings.[[83]](#cite_note-84) 4.9% of people identified as [LGBTIQ+](/wiki/LGBTQ "LGBTQ").[[84]](#cite_note-85) The median age was 35.1 years (compared with 38.1 years nationally). There were 270,384 people (19.3%) aged under 15 years, 307,065 (21.9%) aged 15 to 29, 651,645 (46.5%) aged 30 to 64, and 173,178 (12.3%) aged 65 or older.[[7]](#cite_note-Census_2023-8)

Of those at least 15 years old, 290,814 (25.7%) people had a bachelor's or higher degree, 464,022 (41.0%) had a post-high school certificate or diploma, and 298,851 (26.4%) people exclusively held high school qualifications. The median income was $44,600, compared with $41,500 nationally. 160,164 people (14.2%) earned over $100,000 compared to 12.1% nationally. The employment status of those at least 15 was that 605,601 (53.5%) people were employed full-time, 132,180 (11.7%) were part-time, and 39,441 (3.5%) were unemployed.[[7]](#cite_note-Census_2023-8)

### Culture and identity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auckland&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Culture and identity")]

[![Lion dancers wearing bright red and yellow costumes](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Lion_dancers_at_the_Auckland_lantern_festival_2010.jpg/250px-Lion_dancers_at_the_Auckland_lantern_festival_2010.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lion_dancers_at_the_Auckland_lantern_festival_2010.jpg)

Asians are Auckland's fastest growing ethnic group. Here, [lion dancers](/wiki/Lion_dance "Lion dance") perform at the Auckland [Lantern Festival](/wiki/Lantern_Festival "Lantern Festival").

[Many ethnic groups](/wiki/List_of_ethnic_origins_of_New_Zealanders "List of ethnic origins of New Zealanders"), since the late 20th century, have had an increasing presence in Auckland, making it by far the country's most [cosmopolitan](/wiki/Cosmopolitanism "Cosmopolitanism") city. Historically, Auckland's population has been of majority [European](/wiki/European_New_Zealanders "European New Zealanders") origin, though the proportion of those of Asian or other non-European origins has increased in recent decades due to the [removal of restrictions directly or indirectly based on race](/wiki/Immigration_to_New_Zealand "Immigration to New Zealand"). Europeans continue to make up the plurality of the city's population, but no longer constitute a majority after decreasing in proportion from 54.6% to 48.1% between the 2013 and 2018 censuses. [Asians](/wiki/Asian_New_Zealanders "Asian New Zealanders") now form the second-largest ethnic group, making up nearly one-third of the population. Auckland is home to the largest ethnic [Polynesian](/wiki/Polynesia "Polynesia") population of any city in the world, with a sizeable population of [Pacific Islanders](/wiki/Pacific_Islander "Pacific Islander") ([Pasifika](/wiki/Pasifika_New_Zealanders "Pasifika New Zealanders")) and indigenous [Māori people](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people").[[9]](#cite_note-AAAROUND-10)[[85]](#cite_note-:02-86)

In the 2023 census, where people could identify as more than one ethnicity, the results were 44.0% [European](/wiki/European_New_Zealanders "European New Zealanders") ([Pākehā](/wiki/P%C4%81keh%C4%81 "Pākehā")); 12.2% [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people"); 18.7% [Pasifika](/wiki/Pasifika_New_Zealanders "Pasifika New Zealanders"); 34.9% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_New_Zealanders "Asian New Zealanders"); 2.9% Middle Eastern, Latin American and African New Zealanders (MELAA); and 1.7% other, which includes people giving their ethnicity as "New Zealander". English was spoken by 91.5%, Māori language by 2.7%, Samoan by 5.3% and other languages by 32.0%. No language could be spoken by 2.4% (e.g. too young to talk). [New Zealand Sign Language](/wiki/New_Zealand_Sign_Language "New Zealand Sign Language") was known by 0.4%. The percentage of people born overseas was 44.9, compared with 28.8% nationally.[[7]](#cite_note-Census_2023-8)

At the [2023 census](/wiki/2023_New_Zealand_census "2023 New Zealand census") the Pasifika population formed the majority in the [Māngere-Ōtāhuhu local board area](/wiki/M%C4%81ngere-%C5%8Ct%C4%81huhu "Māngere-Ōtāhuhu") and the plurality in the [Ōtara-Papatoetoe](/wiki/%C5%8Ctara-Papatoetoe "Ōtara-Papatoetoe") and [Manurewa](/wiki/Manurewa_(local_board_area) "Manurewa (local board area)") local board areas. The Asian population formed the majority in the [Howick](/wiki/Howick_(local_board_area) "Howick (local board area)") and [Puketāpapa](/wiki/Puket%C4%81papa_(local_board_area) "Puketāpapa (local board area)") local board areas and the plurality in the [Whau](/wiki/Whau_(local_board_area) "Whau (local board area)") local board area. Europeans formed the plurality in the [Henderson-Massey](/wiki/Henderson-Massey "Henderson-Massey"), [Maungakiekie-Tāmaki](/wiki/Maungakiekie-T%C4%81maki "Maungakiekie-Tāmaki") and [Papakura](/wiki/Papakura_(local_board_area) "Papakura (local board area)") local board areas, and formed the majority in the remaining 11 local board areas. Māori did not form a majority or plurality in any local board area but are in the highest concentrations in the Manurewa and Papakura local board areas.[[86]](#cite_note-:022-87)

Immigration to New Zealand is heavily concentrated towards Auckland (partly for job market reasons). This strong focus on Auckland has led the immigration services to award extra points towards immigration visa requirements for people intending to move to other parts of New Zealand.[[87]](#cite_note-88) Immigration from overseas into Auckland is partially offset by the net emigration of people from Auckland to other regions of New Zealand.[[88]](#cite_note-89) In 2021 and 2022, Auckland recorded its only decreases in population, primarily due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_New_Zealand "COVID-19 pandemic in New Zealand") and the associated lack of international migration.[[89]](#cite_note-90)[[90]](#cite_note-91)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/St_Matthew_In_The_City_Auckland.jpg/250px-St_Matthew_In_The_City_Auckland.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_Matthew_In_The_City_Auckland.jpg)

[St Matthew-in-the-City](/wiki/St_Matthew%27s,_Auckland "St Matthew's, Auckland"), a historic [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican") church in the [Auckland CBD](/wiki/Auckland_CBD "Auckland CBD")

At the 2018 Census, in the local board areas of Upper Harbour, Waitematā, Puketāpapa and Howick, overseas-born residents outnumbered those born in New Zealand.[[91]](#cite_note-quickstats_culture-92) The most common birthplaces of overseas-born residents were mainland China (6.2%), India (4.6%), England (4.4%), Fiji (2.9%), Samoa (2.5%), South Africa (2.4%), Philippines (2.0%), Australia (1.4%), South Korea (1.4%), and Tonga (1.3%).[[92]](#cite_note-:0-93) A study from 2016 showed Auckland has the fourth largest [foreign-born](/wiki/Foreign_born "Foreign born") population in the world, only behind [Dubai](/wiki/Dubai "Dubai"), Toronto and [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"), with 39% of its residents born overseas.[[93]](#cite_note-94)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auckland&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Religion")]

Religious affiliations in the 2023 census were 35.7% [Christian](/wiki/Christianity_in_New_Zealand "Christianity in New Zealand"), 6.1% [Hindu](/wiki/Hinduism_in_New_Zealand "Hinduism in New Zealand"), 3.3% [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_New_Zealand "Islam in New Zealand"), 0.9% [Māori religious beliefs](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_religious_beliefs "Māori religious beliefs"), 2.1% [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhism_in_New_Zealand "Buddhism in New Zealand"), 0.3% [New Age](/wiki/New_Age "New Age"), 0.2% [Jewish](/wiki/Judaism_in_New_Zealand "Judaism in New Zealand"), and 2.8% other religions. People who answered that they had [no religion](/wiki/Irreligion_in_New_Zealand "Irreligion in New Zealand") were 42.7%, and 6.0% of people did not answer the census question. In 2007, immigration from Asia has added to the religious diversity of the city, increasing the number of people affiliating with [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") and [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism"), although there are no figures on religious attendance.[[94]](#cite_note-95) There is also a small, [long-established](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_New_Zealand "History of the Jews in New Zealand") Jewish community.[[95]](#cite_note-96)

### Future growth

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auckland&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Future growth")]

See also: [Demographics of Auckland § Future growth](/wiki/Demographics_of_Auckland#Future_growth "Demographics of Auckland")

Auckland is experiencing substantial population growth via immigration (two-thirds of growth) and natural population increases (one-third),[[96]](#cite_note-ouraucklandgovtnz-97) and is set to grow to an estimated 1.9 million inhabitants by 2031[[97]](#cite_note-ARCGRO-98)[[98]](#cite_note-STATSNZPOP-99) in a medium-variant scenario. This substantial increase in population will have a huge impact on transport, housing and other infrastructure that are, particularly in the case of housing, that are considered to be under pressure already. The high-variant scenario shows the region's population growing to over two million by 2031.[[99]](#cite_note-100)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

In July 2016, Auckland Council released, as the outcome of a three-year study and public hearings, its Unitary Plan for Auckland. The plan aims to free up to 30 percent more land for housing and allows for greater intensification of the existing urban area, creating 422,000 new dwellings in the next 30 years.[[100]](#cite_note-101)

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auckland&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Government")]

### Local

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auckland&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Local")]

Main articles: [Auckland Region](/wiki/Auckland_Region "Auckland Region"), [Auckland Council](/wiki/Auckland_Council "Auckland Council"), and [Mayor of Auckland](/wiki/Mayor_of_Auckland "Mayor of Auckland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Aucklandtownhallnight.jpg/250px-Aucklandtownhallnight.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aucklandtownhallnight.jpg)

[Auckland Town Hall](/wiki/Auckland_Town_Hall "Auckland Town Hall") entrance on [Queen Street](/wiki/Queen_Street,_Auckland "Queen Street, Auckland")

In 1851, Auckland was constituted as an independent borough, the first in the country.[[101]](#cite_note-barr-102): 78 The borough was 58,000 acres (23,000 ha) in size and went as far west as the [Whau River](/wiki/Whau_River "Whau River") and as far east as [Howick](/wiki/Howick,_New_Zealand "Howick, New Zealand"). The large boundary included 4,000 Europeans in the urban areas; 2,500 in the Fencible settlements; and 1,500 in the rural parts. An inability to collect rates and a divide between urban and rural interests led to the borough becoming defunct in 1852. In 1854, the [Auckland Provincial Council](/wiki/Auckland_Provincial_Council "Auckland Provincial Council") established Auckland City under the Auckland City Council Act. This city had boundaries equivalent to the [City of Auckland electorate](/wiki/City_of_Auckland_(New_Zealand_electorate) "City of Auckland (New Zealand electorate)"). The legality of this city was challenged and it was later dissolved in March 1855 before finally being repealed by an act in April 1856. An [Auckland Harbour Board](/wiki/Auckland_Harbour_Board "Auckland Harbour Board") was also established and repealed during the same period. In 1863, the Town of Auckland was incorporated within the same boundaries of the East Town, Middle Town, and West Town wards of the borough.[[102]](#cite_note-103) On 24 April 1871, Auckland became a city under *The Municipal Corporations Act, 1867*.[[101]](#cite_note-barr-102): 80

The Auckland Council is the [local authority](/wiki/Regions_of_New_Zealand "Regions of New Zealand") with jurisdiction over the city of Auckland, along with surrounding rural areas, parkland, and the islands of the Hauraki Gulf.[[103]](#cite_note-104)

From 1989 to 2010, Auckland was governed by several city and district councils, with regional oversight by [Auckland Regional Council](/wiki/Auckland_Regional_Council "Auckland Regional Council"). In the late 2000s, New Zealand's central government and parts of Auckland's society felt that this large number of councils, and the lack of strong regional government (with the Auckland Regional Council having only limited powers), were hindering Auckland's progress.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

A [Royal Commission on Auckland Governance](/wiki/Royal_Commission_on_Auckland_Governance "Royal Commission on Auckland Governance") was set up in 2007;[[104]](#cite_note-105)[[105]](#cite_note-106) in 2009, it recommended a unified local governance structure for Auckland by amalgamating the councils.[[106]](#cite_note-107) The government subsequently announced that a "super city" would be set up with a single mayor by the time of New Zealand's local body elections in 2010.[[107]](#cite_note-108)[[108]](#cite_note-109)

In October 2010, [Manukau City](/wiki/Manukau_City "Manukau City") mayor [Len Brown](/wiki/Len_Brown "Len Brown") was elected mayor of the amalgamated [Auckland Council](/wiki/Auckland_Council "Auckland Council"). He was re-elected for a second term in October 2013. Brown did not stand for re-election in the [2016 mayoral election](/wiki/2016_Auckland_mayoral_election "2016 Auckland mayoral election"), and was succeeded by successful candidate [Phil Goff](/wiki/Phil_Goff "Phil Goff") in October 2016.[[109]](#cite_note-110) Twenty councillors make up the remainder of the Auckland Council governing body, elected from thirteen electoral wards.

### National

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auckland&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: National")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Old_Government_House_in_Auckland.jpg/250px-Old_Government_House_in_Auckland.jpg)](/wiki/File:Old_Government_House_in_Auckland.jpg)

[Old Government House](/wiki/Old_Government_House,_Auckland "Old Government House, Auckland"), formerly the residence of the [governor of New Zealand](/wiki/Governor-General_of_New_Zealand "Governor-General of New Zealand")

Between 1842 and 1865, Auckland was the [capital city of New Zealand](/wiki/Capital_of_New_Zealand "Capital of New Zealand").[[110]](#cite_note-111) Parliament met in what is now [Old Government House](/wiki/Old_Government_House,_Auckland "Old Government House, Auckland") on the [University of Auckland](/wiki/University_of_Auckland "University of Auckland")'s City campus. The capital was moved to the more centrally located [Wellington](/wiki/Wellington "Wellington") in 1865.

Auckland, due to its large population, is covered by 23 general electorates and three [Māori electorates](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_electorates "Māori electorates"),[[111]](#cite_note-112) each returning one member to the [New Zealand House of Representatives](/wiki/New_Zealand_House_of_Representatives "New Zealand House of Representatives"). The [National Party](/wiki/New_Zealand_National_Party "New Zealand National Party") holds 14 general electorates, the [Labour Party](/wiki/New_Zealand_Labour_Party "New Zealand Labour Party") six, [ACT](/wiki/ACT_New_Zealand "ACT New Zealand") two and the [Greens](/wiki/Green_Party_of_Aotearoa_New_Zealand "Green Party of Aotearoa New Zealand") one. Two of the three Māori electorates are held by [Te Pāti Māori](/wiki/Te_P%C4%81ti_M%C4%81ori "Te Pāti Māori"); the remaining Māori electorate is held by an independent.

In 1891, the three main items exported from Auckland's port were: [kauri gum](/wiki/Kauri_gum "Kauri gum"), gold, and wool. These exports were collectively worth almost a million pounds.[[112]](#cite_note-113)

Auckland is the major economic and financial centre of New Zealand. It has an advanced [market economy](/wiki/Market_economy "Market economy") with strengths in finance, commerce, and tourism. Most major international corporations have an Auckland office; the most expensive office space is around lower [Queen Street](/wiki/Queen_Street,_Auckland "Queen Street, Auckland") and the [Viaduct Basin](/wiki/Viaduct_Basin "Viaduct Basin") in the [Auckland CBD](/wiki/Auckland_CBD "Auckland CBD"), where many financial and business services are located, which constitute a large percentage of the CBD economy.[[113]](#cite_note-GLANCE-114) The largest commercial and industrial areas of the Auckland Region are Auckland CBD and the western parts of [Manukau](/wiki/Manukau "Manukau"), mostly bordering the [Manukau Harbour](/wiki/Manukau_Harbour "Manukau Harbour") and the [Tāmaki River](/wiki/T%C4%81maki_River "Tāmaki River") estuary.

According to the 2013 census, the primary employment industries of Auckland residents are professional, scientific and technical services (11.4 percent), manufacturing (9.9 percent), retail trade (9.7 percent), health care and social assistance (9.1 percent), and education and training (8.3 percent). Manufacturing is the largest employer in the Henderson-Massey, Howick, Māngere-Ōtāhuhu, Ōtara-Papatoetoe, Manurewa and Papakura local board areas, retail trade is the largest employer in the Whau local board area, while professional, scientific and technical services are the largest employer in the remaining urban local board areas.[[114]](#cite_note-115)

The GDP of Auckland in 2024 was estimated at approximately $159.74 billion NZD, which saw a 2.1% increase from 2023.[[115]](#cite_note-116) The per-capita GDP of Auckland was estimated at $88,878 at the end of 2024, the second-highest in the country after the Wellington region, and above the national average of $78,233.[[116]](#cite_note-117)[[117]](#cite_note-118)

Landmark House

Auckland has a diversity of architectural styles, resulting from its early beginnings as a settlement, the [Victorian](/wiki/Victorian_architecture "Victorian architecture") period, the 20th century and through to the contemporary era of the 21st century. Legislation is in effect to protect the remaining heritage, with the key piece being the Resource Management Act of 1991.[[118]](#cite_note-119) Prepared under this legislation is the Auckland Unitary Plan, which indicates how land can be used or developed. Prominent historic buildings in Auckland include the [Dilworth Building](/wiki/Dilworth_Building "Dilworth Building"), the [Auckland Ferry Terminal](/wiki/Auckland_Ferry_Terminal "Auckland Ferry Terminal"), Guardian Trust Building, Old Customs House, Landmark House, the [Auckland Town Hall](/wiki/Auckland_Town_Hall "Auckland Town Hall") and the [Britomart Transport Centre](/wiki/Britomart_Transport_Centre "Britomart Transport Centre") – many of these are located on Queen Street, the main street.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Housing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auckland&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Housing")]

See also: [Housing in New Zealand](/wiki/Housing_in_New_Zealand "Housing in New Zealand") and [New Zealand property bubble](/wiki/New_Zealand_property_bubble "New Zealand property bubble")

Housing varies considerably, between suburbs that have [state-owned housing](/wiki/State_housing_in_New_Zealand "State housing in New Zealand") in lower income neighbourhoods, to larger waterfront homes, especially in areas close to the [Waitematā Harbour](/wiki/Waitemat%C4%81_Harbour "Waitematā Harbour"). Historically, the most common residence of Aucklanders was a standalone dwelling on a '[quarter acre](/wiki/Quarter_acre "Quarter acre")' (1,000 m2).[[97]](#cite_note-ARCGRO-98) However, subdividing such properties with 'infill housing' has long been the norm. Auckland's housing stock has become more diverse in recent decades, with many more apartments being built since the 1970s, particularly since the 1990s in the CBD.[[119]](#cite_note-DBHreport-120) Nevertheless, the majority of Aucklanders live in single dwelling housing and are expected to continue to do so, even with most of future urban growth being through intensification.[[97]](#cite_note-ARCGRO-98)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Terrace_Houses_Auckland.jpg/250px-Terrace_Houses_Auckland.jpg)](/wiki/File:Terrace_Houses_Auckland.jpg)

[Terraced housing](/wiki/Terraced_housing "Terraced housing") built in 1897 as residential buildings and associated place houses for [John Endean](/wiki/John_Endean "John Endean")[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

Auckland has been described as having "the most extensive range of timbered housing with its classical details and mouldings in the world", many of them built in the Victorian and Edwardian eras.[[120]](#cite_note-121) In some areas, the Victorian [villas](/wiki/Villa "Villa") have been torn down to make way for redevelopment. The demolition of the older houses is being combated through increased heritage protection for older parts of the city.[[121]](#cite_note-122)

Auckland's housing is amongst the least affordable in the world, based on comparing average house prices with average household income levels[[122]](#cite_note-123)[[123]](#cite_note-124) and house prices have grown well above the rate of inflation in recent decades.[[119]](#cite_note-DBHreport-120) In August 2022, the Real Estate Institute of New Zealand (REINZ) reported the median house price in the Auckland Region was $1,100,000, ranging from $900,000 in the former Papakura District area to $1,285,000 in the former North Shore City area, This is compared to a median price of $700,000 outside of Auckland.[[124]](#cite_note-125) There is significant public debate around why Auckland's housing is so expensive, often referring to a lack of land supply,[[119]](#cite_note-DBHreport-120) the easy availability of credit for residential investment[[125]](#cite_note-bloomberg-126) and Auckland's high level of liveability.

In the lead-up to 2010, a housing crisis began in Auckland, with the market not being able to sustain the demand for affordable homes. The Housing Accords and Special Housing Areas Act 2013 mandated that a minimum of 10 percent of new builds in certain housing areas be subsidised to make them affordable for buyers who had incomes on par with the national average. In a new subdivision at [Hobsonville Point](/wiki/Hobsonville "Hobsonville"), 20 percent of new homes were reduced to below $550,000.[[126]](#cite_note-127) Some of the demand for new housing at this time was attributed to the 43,000 people who moved into Auckland between June 2014 and June 2015.[[96]](#cite_note-ouraucklandgovtnz-97) Research has found that Auckland is set to become even more densely populated in future which could ease the burden by creating higher density housing in the city centre.[[127]](#cite_note-128)[[128]](#cite_note-Muhammad-129) From around November 2021 to May 2022, house prices dropped 11.68%.[[129]](#cite_note-130) It has continued to fall since due to inflation, bank interest rates, and a variety of other factors.[[130]](#cite_note-131)[[131]](#cite_note-132)[[132]](#cite_note-133)[[133]](#cite_note-134)

Pedestrians on Vulcan Lane in the CBD

Positive aspects of Auckland life are its mild climate, plentiful employment and educational opportunities, as well as numerous leisure facilities. Meanwhile, traffic problems, the lack of good public transport, and increasing housing costs have been cited by many Aucklanders as among the strongest negative factors of living there,[[134]](#cite_note-criticisms-135) together with crime that has been rising in recent years.[[135]](#cite_note-136) Nonetheless, Auckland ranked third in a survey of the quality of life of 215 major cities of the world (2015 data).[[136]](#cite_note-top_cities-137)

### Leisure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auckland&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Leisure")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Two_sailboats_on_the_main_Takapuna_boat_ramp.jpg/250px-Two_sailboats_on_the_main_Takapuna_boat_ramp.jpg)](/wiki/File:Two_sailboats_on_the_main_Takapuna_boat_ramp.jpg)

Sailboats at [Takapuna](/wiki/Takapuna "Takapuna") Beach on the [North Shore](/wiki/North_Shore_City "North Shore City")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Auckland%2C_New_Zealand%2C_11_Nov._2010_-_Flickr_-_PhillipC.jpg/250px-Auckland%2C_New_Zealand%2C_11_Nov._2010_-_Flickr_-_PhillipC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Auckland,_New_Zealand,_11_Nov._2010_-_Flickr_-_PhillipC.jpg)

Yachts docked in [Westhaven Marina](/wiki/Westhaven_Marina "Westhaven Marina") on the [Waitematā Harbour](/wiki/Waitemat%C4%81_Harbour "Waitematā Harbour")

One of Auckland's nicknames, the "City of Sails", is derived from the popularity of sailing in the region.[[21]](#cite_note-City_of_Sails-22) 135,000 [yachts](/wiki/Yacht "Yacht") and [launches](/wiki/Launch_(boat) "Launch (boat)") are registered in Auckland, and around 60,500 of the country's 149,900 registered yachtsmen are from Auckland,[[137]](#cite_note-Heraldyacht-138) with about one in three Auckland households owning a boat.[[138]](#cite_note-LIVINGGULF-139) The [Viaduct Basin](/wiki/Viaduct_Basin "Viaduct Basin"), on the western edge of the CBD, hosted three [America's Cup](/wiki/America%27s_Cup "America's Cup") challenges ([2000 Cup](/wiki/2000_America%27s_Cup "2000 America's Cup"), [2003 Cup](/wiki/2003_America%27s_Cup "2003 America's Cup") and [2021 Cup](/wiki/2021_America%27s_Cup "2021 America's Cup")).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [Waitematā Harbour](/wiki/Waitemat%C4%81_Harbour "Waitematā Harbour") is home to several notable yacht clubs and marinas, including the [Royal New Zealand Yacht Squadron](/wiki/Royal_New_Zealand_Yacht_Squadron "Royal New Zealand Yacht Squadron") and [Westhaven Marina](/wiki/Westhaven_Marina "Westhaven Marina"), the largest of the [Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere").[[137]](#cite_note-Heraldyacht-138) The Waitematā Harbour has several swimming beaches, including [Mission Bay](/wiki/Mission_Bay,_New_Zealand "Mission Bay, New Zealand") and [Kohimarama](/wiki/Kohimarama "Kohimarama") on the south side of the harbour, and Stanley Bay on the north side. On the eastern coastline of the North Shore, where the Rangitoto Channel divides the inner Hauraki Gulf islands from the mainland, there are popular swimming beaches at Cheltenham and Narrow Neck in [Devonport](/wiki/Devonport,_New_Zealand "Devonport, New Zealand"), [Takapuna](/wiki/Takapuna "Takapuna"), [Milford](/wiki/Milford,_New_Zealand "Milford, New Zealand"), and the various beaches further north in the area known as East Coast Bays.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The west coast has popular surf beaches such as [Piha](/wiki/Piha "Piha"), [Muriwai](/wiki/Muriwai "Muriwai") and [Te Henga (Bethells Beach)](/wiki/Te_Henga_(Bethells_Beach) "Te Henga (Bethells Beach)"). The [Whangaparāoa Peninsula](/wiki/Whangapar%C4%81oa_Peninsula "Whangaparāoa Peninsula"), [Orewa](/wiki/Orewa "Orewa"), [Ōmaha](/wiki/%C5%8Cmaha "Ōmaha") and [Pākiri](/wiki/P%C4%81kiri "Pākiri"), to the north of the main urban area, are also nearby. Many Auckland beaches are patrolled by [surf lifesaving](/wiki/Surf_lifesaving "Surf lifesaving") clubs, such as [Piha Surf Life Saving Club](/wiki/Piha_Surf_Life_Saving_Club "Piha Surf Life Saving Club") the home of [Piha Rescue](/wiki/Piha_Rescue "Piha Rescue"). All surf lifesaving clubs are part of the [Surf Life Saving Northern Region](/wiki/Surf_Life_Saving_Northern_Region "Surf Life Saving Northern Region").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Shopping

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auckland&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Shopping")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Sylvia_Park_Parking_And_Mall.jpg/250px-Sylvia_Park_Parking_And_Mall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sylvia_Park_Parking_And_Mall.jpg)

[Sylvia Park](/wiki/Sylvia_Park "Sylvia Park")

[Queen Street](/wiki/Queen_Street,_Auckland "Queen Street, Auckland"), [Britomart](/wiki/Britomart_Transport_Centre "Britomart Transport Centre"), [Ponsonby Road](/wiki/Ponsonby,_New_Zealand "Ponsonby, New Zealand"), [Karangahape Road](/wiki/Karangahape_Road "Karangahape Road"), [Newmarket](/wiki/Newmarket,_New_Zealand "Newmarket, New Zealand") and [Parnell](/wiki/Parnell,_New_Zealand "Parnell, New Zealand") are major retail areas. Major markets include those held in [Ōtara](/wiki/%C5%8Ctara "Ōtara") and [Avondale](/wiki/Avondale,_Auckland "Avondale, Auckland") on weekend mornings. A number of shopping centres are located in the middle- and outer-suburbs, with [Westfield Newmarket](/wiki/Westfield_Newmarket "Westfield Newmarket"), [Sylvia Park](/wiki/Sylvia_Park "Sylvia Park"), [Botany Town Centre](/wiki/Botany_Town_Centre "Botany Town Centre") and [Westfield Albany](/wiki/Westfield_Albany "Westfield Albany") being the largest.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Arts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auckland&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Arts")]

A number of arts events are held in Auckland, including the [Auckland Festival](/wiki/Auckland_Festival "Auckland Festival"), the Auckland Triennial, the [New Zealand International Comedy Festival](/wiki/New_Zealand_International_Comedy_Festival "New Zealand International Comedy Festival"), and the [New Zealand International Film Festival](/wiki/New_Zealand_International_Film_Festival "New Zealand International Film Festival"). The [Auckland Philharmonia](/wiki/Auckland_Philharmonia "Auckland Philharmonia") is the city and region's resident full-time symphony orchestra, performing its own series of concerts and accompanying opera and ballet. Events celebrating the city's cultural diversity include the [Pasifika Festival](/wiki/Pasifika_Festival "Pasifika Festival"), the Auckland Secondary Schools Māori & Pacific Islands Cultural Festival (more commonly known as Polyfest), and the [Auckland Lantern Festival](/wiki/Lantern_Festival "Lantern Festival"), all of which are the largest of their kind in New Zealand. Additionally, Auckland regularly hosts the [New Zealand Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/New_Zealand_Symphony_Orchestra "New Zealand Symphony Orchestra") and [Royal New Zealand Ballet](/wiki/Royal_New_Zealand_Ballet "Royal New Zealand Ballet"). Auckland is part of the [UNESCO Creative Cities Network](/wiki/Creative_Cities_Network "Creative Cities Network") in the category of music.[[139]](#cite_note-140)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Auckland_Art_Gallery_-_Extension.jpg/250px-Auckland_Art_Gallery_-_Extension.jpg)](/wiki/File:Auckland_Art_Gallery_-_Extension.jpg)

The modern section of the [Auckland Art Gallery](/wiki/Auckland_Art_Gallery "Auckland Art Gallery"), completed in 2011

Important institutions include the [Auckland Art Gallery](/wiki/Auckland_Art_Gallery "Auckland Art Gallery"), [Auckland War Memorial Museum](/wiki/Auckland_War_Memorial_Museum "Auckland War Memorial Museum"), [New Zealand Maritime Museum](/wiki/New_Zealand_Maritime_Museum "New Zealand Maritime Museum"), [National Museum of the Royal New Zealand Navy](/wiki/Torpedo_Bay_Navy_Museum "Torpedo Bay Navy Museum"), and the [Museum of Transport and Technology](/wiki/Museum_of_Transport_and_Technology "Museum of Transport and Technology"). The Auckland Art Gallery is the largest stand-alone gallery in New Zealand with a collection of over 17,000 artworks, including prominent New Zealand and Pacific Island artists, as well as international painting, sculpture and print collections ranging in date from 1376 to the present day.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Auckland_War_Memorial_Museum_Front_View_2023.jpg/250px-Auckland_War_Memorial_Museum_Front_View_2023.jpg)](/wiki/File:Auckland_War_Memorial_Museum_Front_View_2023.jpg)

[Auckland War Memorial Museum](/wiki/Auckland_War_Memorial_Museum "Auckland War Memorial Museum")

Other important art galleries include [Mangere Arts Centre](/wiki/M%C4%81ngere_Arts_Centre_-_Ng%C4%81_Tohu_o_Uenuku "Māngere Arts Centre - Ngā Tohu o Uenuku"), [Tautai Pacific Arts Trust](/wiki/Tautai_Pacific_Arts_Trust "Tautai Pacific Arts Trust"), [Te Tuhi](/wiki/Te_Tuhi "Te Tuhi"), [Te Uru Waitākere Contemporary Gallery](/wiki/Te_Uru_Wait%C4%81kere_Contemporary_Gallery "Te Uru Waitākere Contemporary Gallery"), [Gow Langsford Gallery](/wiki/Gow_Langsford_Gallery "Gow Langsford Gallery"), Michael Lett Gallery, Starkwhite, and [Bergman Gallery](/wiki/Bergman_Gallery "Bergman Gallery").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Auckland Domain](/wiki/Auckland_Domain "Auckland Domain") is one of the largest parks in the city. It is close to the [Auckland CBD](/wiki/Auckland_CBD "Auckland CBD"). Smaller parks close to the city centre are [Albert Park](/wiki/Albert_Park,_Auckland "Albert Park, Auckland"), [Myers Park](/wiki/Myers_Park,_Auckland "Myers Park, Auckland"), [Western Park](/wiki/Western_Park,_Auckland "Western Park, Auckland") and [Victoria Park](/wiki/Victoria_Park,_Auckland "Victoria Park, Auckland").

While most volcanic cones in the [Auckland volcanic field](/wiki/Auckland_volcanic_field "Auckland volcanic field") have been affected by quarrying, many of the remaining cones are now within parks, and retain a more natural character than the surrounding city. Prehistoric earthworks and historic fortifications are in several of these parks, including [Maungawhau / Mount Eden](/wiki/Mount_Eden "Mount Eden"), [North Head](/wiki/North_Head,_New_Zealand "North Head, New Zealand") and [Maungakiekie / One Tree Hill](/wiki/One_Tree_Hill,_New_Zealand "One Tree Hill, New Zealand").

Other parks around the city are in [Western Springs Reserve](/wiki/Western_Springs_Reserve "Western Springs Reserve"), which has a large park bordering the [MOTAT](/wiki/MOTAT "MOTAT") museum and the [Auckland Zoo](/wiki/Auckland_Zoo "Auckland Zoo"). The [Auckland Botanic Gardens](/wiki/Auckland_Botanic_Gardens "Auckland Botanic Gardens") are further south, in [Manurewa](/wiki/Manurewa "Manurewa").

Ferries provide transport to parks and nature reserves at [Devonport](/wiki/Devonport,_New_Zealand "Devonport, New Zealand"), [Waiheke Island](/wiki/Waiheke_Island "Waiheke Island"), Rangitoto Island and [Tiritiri Matangi](/wiki/Tiritiri_Matangi "Tiritiri Matangi"). The [Waitākere Ranges](/wiki/Wait%C4%81kere_Ranges "Waitākere Ranges") Regional Park to the west of Auckland has relatively unspoiled [bush](/wiki/The_Bush#New_Zealand "The Bush") territory, as do the [Hunua Ranges](/wiki/Hunua_Ranges "Hunua Ranges") to the south.

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auckland&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Transport")]

Main articles: [Transport in Auckland](/wiki/Transport_in_Auckland "Transport in Auckland") and [Public transport in Auckland](/wiki/Public_transport_in_Auckland "Public transport in Auckland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Britomart_Outside_Facade.jpg/250px-Britomart_Outside_Facade.jpg)](/wiki/File:Britomart_Outside_Facade.jpg)

Railway lines serve the western, southern and eastern parts of the city from the [Waitematā railway station](/wiki/Waitemat%C4%81_railway_station "Waitematā railway station"), also known as Britomart.

During the 19th century the main forms of transport to and from Auckland were ferries and trains, with horses being used for shorter distances and trips within the city. From 1902 electric trams provided transport for the central isthmus and resulted in residential expansion in the area. Following the First World War car ownership started to increase and concrete and bitumen roads were built to accommodate this.[[140]](#cite_note-141)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Southern_Motorway_Auckland_traffic_-_copyright-free_photo_released_to_public_domain.jpg/250px-Southern_Motorway_Auckland_traffic_-_copyright-free_photo_released_to_public_domain.jpg)](/wiki/File:Southern_Motorway_Auckland_traffic_-_copyright-free_photo_released_to_public_domain.jpg)

Traffic on the [Southern Motorway](/wiki/Auckland_Southern_Motorway "Auckland Southern Motorway")

The [State Highway network](/wiki/New_Zealand_state_highway_network "New Zealand state highway network") connects the different parts of Auckland, with [State Highway 1](/wiki/State_Highway_1_(New_Zealand) "State Highway 1 (New Zealand)") the major north–south thoroughfare through the city (including both the [Northern](/wiki/Auckland_Northern_Motorway "Auckland Northern Motorway") and [Southern Motorways](/wiki/Auckland_Southern_Motorway "Auckland Southern Motorway")) and the main connection to the adjoining regions of [Northland](/wiki/Northland_region "Northland region") and [Waikato](/wiki/Waikato "Waikato"). The [Northern Busway](/wiki/Northern_Busway,_Auckland "Northern Busway, Auckland") runs alongside part of the Northern Motorway on the North Shore. Other state highways within Auckland include [State Highway 16](/wiki/New_Zealand_State_Highway_16 "New Zealand State Highway 16") (the Northwest Motorway), [State Highway 18](/wiki/New_Zealand_State_Highway_18 "New Zealand State Highway 18") (the Upper Harbour Motorway) and [State Highway 20](/wiki/New_Zealand_State_Highway_20 "New Zealand State Highway 20") (the Southwest Motorway). [State Highway 22](/wiki/New_Zealand_State_Highway_22 "New Zealand State Highway 22") is a non-motorway rural arterial connecting [Pukekohe](/wiki/Pukekohe "Pukekohe") to the Southern Motorway at [Drury](/wiki/Drury,_New_Zealand "Drury, New Zealand").[[141]](#cite_note-Waka_Kotahi-142)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Auckland_Harbour_Bridge_aerial.jpg/330px-Auckland_Harbour_Bridge_aerial.jpg)](/wiki/File:Auckland_Harbour_Bridge_aerial.jpg)

Aerial view of the Auckland Harbour Bridge

The [Auckland Harbour Bridge](/wiki/Auckland_Harbour_Bridge "Auckland Harbour Bridge"), opened in 1959, is the main connection between the [North Shore](/wiki/North_Shore,_New_Zealand "North Shore, New Zealand") and the rest of Auckland.[[142]](#cite_note-143) The bridge provides eight lanes of vehicle traffic and has a moveable median barrier for lane flexibility, but does not provide access for rail, pedestrians or cyclists. The [Central Motorway Junction](/wiki/Central_Motorway_Junction "Central Motorway Junction"), also called 'Spaghetti Junction' for its complexity, is the intersection between the two major motorways of Auckland (State Highway 1 and State Highway 16).[[143]](#cite_note-144)

Two of the longest arterial roads within the Auckland Region are [Great North Road](/wiki/Great_North_Road,_Auckland "Great North Road, Auckland") and [Great South Road](/wiki/Great_South_Road,_New_Zealand "Great South Road, New Zealand") – the main connections in those directions before the construction of the State Highway network.[[141]](#cite_note-Waka_Kotahi-142) Numerous arterial roads also provide regional and sub-regional connectivity, with many of these roads (especially on the isthmus) previously used to operate Auckland's [former tram network](/wiki/Trams_in_New_Zealand#Auckland "Trams in New Zealand").

Auckland has four railway lines ([Western](/wiki/Western_Line_(Auckland) "Western Line (Auckland)"), [Onehunga](/wiki/Onehunga_Line "Onehunga Line"), [Eastern](/wiki/Eastern_Line_(Auckland) "Eastern Line (Auckland)") and [Southern](/wiki/Southern_Line_(Auckland) "Southern Line (Auckland)")). These lines serve the western, southern and eastern parts of Auckland from the [Waitematā railway station](/wiki/Britomart_Transport_Centre "Britomart Transport Centre") in downtown Auckland, the terminal station for all lines, where connections are also available to ferry and bus services. Work began in late 2015 to provide more route flexibility and connect Britomart, now named Waitematā, more directly to the western suburbs on the Western Line via an underground rail tunnel known as the [City Rail Link](/wiki/City_Rail_Link "City Rail Link") project. A plan for a [light rail network](/wiki/Light_rail_in_Auckland "Light rail in Auckland") was cancelled in 2024.[[144]](#cite_note-RNZ_1-145)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Auckland_airport_international_terminal.jpg/250px-Auckland_airport_international_terminal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Auckland_airport_international_terminal.jpg)

[Auckland Airport](/wiki/Auckland_Airport "Auckland Airport")'s international terminal

[Auckland Airport](/wiki/Auckland_Airport "Auckland Airport"), New Zealand's largest and busiest, is in the southern suburb of Māngere on the shores of the Manukau Harbour. It services both domestic and international flights. There are also several small regional airports.

[Auckland's ports](/wiki/Ports_of_Auckland "Ports of Auckland") are the second largest in the country, behind the [Port of Tauranga](/wiki/Port_of_Tauranga "Port of Tauranga"),[[145]](#cite_note-146) and a large part of both inbound and outbound New Zealand commerce travels through them, mostly via the facilities northeast of Auckland CBD. Freight usually arrives at or is distributed from the port via road, though the port facilities also have rail access. Auckland is a major cruise ship stopover point, with the ships usually tying up at [Princes Wharf](/wiki/Princes_Wharf,_Auckland "Princes Wharf, Auckland"). Auckland CBD is connected to the coastal suburbs, to the North Shore and to outlying islands by ferry.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Travel modes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auckland&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Travel modes")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/AMA_103_at_Puhinui.jpg/250px-AMA_103_at_Puhinui.jpg)](/wiki/File:AMA_103_at_Puhinui.jpg)
