[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Devonport_Wharf_Kea_Ferry.jpg/250px-Devonport_Wharf_Kea_Ferry.jpg)](/wiki/File:Devonport_Wharf_Kea_Ferry.jpg)

Devonport ferry terminal

Private vehicles are the main form of transportation within Auckland, with around seven percent of journeys in the Auckland Region undertaken by bus in 2006,[[146]](#cite_note-TRAPLAN-147) and two percent undertaken by train and ferry.[[146]](#cite_note-TRAPLAN-147) For trips to the city centre at peak times, the use of public transport is much higher, with more than half of trips undertaken by bus, train or ferry.[[147]](#cite_note-arta.co.nz-148) In 2010, Auckland ranked quite low in its use of public transport, having only 46 public transport trips per capita per year,[[147]](#cite_note-arta.co.nz-148)[[148]](#cite_note-149) while Wellington has almost twice this number at 91, and Sydney has 114 trips.[[149]](#cite_note-150) This strong dependence on roads results in substantial [traffic congestion](/wiki/Traffic_congestion "Traffic congestion") during peak times.[[150]](#cite_note-NIGHT-151) This car reliance means 56% of the city's energy usage goes towards transportation, and CO2 emissions will increase by 20% in the next 10 years.[[128]](#cite_note-Muhammad-129)

Bus services in Auckland consist mostly of radial and cross-town routes. Late-night services (i.e. past midnight) are limited, even on weekends. A major overhaul of Auckland's bus services was implemented during 2016–18, significantly expanding the reach of frequent bus services: those that operate at least every 15 minutes during the day and early evening, every day of the week.[[151]](#cite_note-152) Auckland is connected with other cities through bus services operated by [InterCity](/wiki/InterCity_(New_Zealand) "InterCity (New Zealand)").

Rail services operate along four lines between the CBD and the west, south and south-east of Auckland, with longer-distance trains operating to Wellington only a few times each week.[[152]](#cite_note-153) Following the opening of [Waitematā railway station](/wiki/Britomart_Transport_Centre "Britomart Transport Centre") in 2003, major investment in Auckland's rail network occurred, involving station upgrades, rolling stock refurbishment and infrastructure improvements.[[153]](#cite_note-154) The rail upgrade has included [electrification of Auckland's rail network](/wiki/Auckland_railway_electrification "Auckland railway electrification"), with electric trains constructed by [Construcciones y Auxiliar de Ferrocarriles](/wiki/Construcciones_y_Auxiliar_de_Ferrocarriles "Construcciones y Auxiliar de Ferrocarriles") commencing service in April 2014.[[154]](#cite_note-155) A number of proposed projects to further extend Auckland's rail network were included in the 2012 Auckland Plan, including the [City Rail Link](/wiki/City_Rail_Link "City Rail Link"), the [Auckland Airport Line](/wiki/Auckland_Airport_Line "Auckland Airport Line"), the [Avondale-Southdown Line](/wiki/Avondale-Southdown_Line "Avondale-Southdown Line") and rail to the North Shore.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Research at [Griffith University](/wiki/Griffith_University "Griffith University") has indicated that from the 1950s to the 1980s, Auckland engaged in some of the most pro-automobile transport policies anywhere in the world.[[155]](#cite_note-GRIFF-156) With public transport declining heavily during the second half of the 20th century (a trend mirrored in most Western countries, such as the US),[[156]](#cite_note-157) and increased spending on roads and cars, New Zealand (and specifically Auckland) now has the second-highest vehicle ownership rate in the world, with around 578 vehicles per 1000 people.[[157]](#cite_note-vehicle_ownership-158) Auckland has also been called a very pedestrian- and cyclist-unfriendly city, though some efforts are being made to change this,[[158]](#cite_note-Big_steps_to_change_City_of_Cars-159) with Auckland being a major participant in the government's "Urban Cycleways" initiative, and with the "SkyPath" project for a walk and cycleway on the Auckland Harbour Bridge having received Council support, and planning consent.[[159]](#cite_note-160)[[160]](#cite_note-161)

## Infrastructure and services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auckland&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Infrastructure and services")]

### Electricity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auckland&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Electricity")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Otahuhu_B_combined_cycle_turbine.jpg/250px-Otahuhu_B_combined_cycle_turbine.jpg)](/wiki/File:Otahuhu_B_combined_cycle_turbine.jpg)

[Ōtāhuhu Power Station](/wiki/Otahuhu_Power_Station "Otahuhu Power Station")'s 404MW combined cycle turbine, known as Ōtāhuhu B, shutdown in 2015

[Vector](/wiki/Vector_Limited "Vector Limited") owns and operates the majority of the distribution network in urban Auckland,[[161]](#cite_note-162) with Counties Energy owning and operating the network south of central Papakura.[[162]](#cite_note-163) The city is supplied from [Transpower](/wiki/Transpower_New_Zealand_Limited "Transpower New Zealand Limited")'s national grid from thirteen substations across the city. There are no major electricity generation stations located within the city or north of Auckland, so almost all of the electricity for Auckland and Northland must be transmitted from power stations in the south, mainly from [Huntly Power Station](/wiki/Huntly_Power_Station "Huntly Power Station") and the [Waikato River](/wiki/Waikato_River "Waikato River") hydroelectric stations. The city had two natural gas-fired power stations (the 404 MW [Ōtāhuhu B](/wiki/Otahuhu_Power_Station "Otahuhu Power Station") and the 175 MW [Southdown](/wiki/Southdown_Power_Station "Southdown Power Station")), but both shut down in 2015.[[163]](#cite_note-164)[[164]](#cite_note-165)

There have been several notable power outages in Auckland.[[165]](#cite_note-166) The five-week-long [1998 Auckland power crisis](/wiki/1998_Auckland_power_crisis "1998 Auckland power crisis") blacked out much of the CBD after a cascade failure occurred on the four main underground cables supplying the CBD.[[166]](#cite_note-167) The [2006 Auckland Blackout](/wiki/2006_Auckland_Blackout "2006 Auckland Blackout") interrupted supply to the CBD and many inner suburbs after an earth wire shackle at Transpower's Otāhuhu substation broke and short-circuited the lines supplying the inner city.

In 2009, much of the northern and western suburbs, as well as all of [Northland](/wiki/Northland_region "Northland region"), experienced a blackout when a forklift accidentally came into contact with the Ōtāhuhu to Henderson 220 kV line, the only major line supplying the region.[[167]](#cite_note-168) Transpower spent $1.25 billion in the early 2010s reinforcing the supply into and across Auckland, including a [400 kV-capable transmission line](/wiki/Whakamaru_to_Brownhill_Road_transmission_line "Whakamaru to Brownhill Road transmission line") from the Waikato River to Brownhill substation (operating initially at 220 kV), and 220 kV underground cables between Brownhill and Pakuranga, and between [Pakuranga and Albany via the CBD](/wiki/North_Auckland_and_Northland_grid_upgrade_project "North Auckland and Northland grid upgrade project"). These reduced the Auckland Region's reliance on Ōtāhuhu substation and northern and western Auckland's reliance on the Ōtāhuhu to Henderson line.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Natural gas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auckland&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Natural gas")]

Auckland was one of the original nine towns and cities in New Zealand to be supplied with natural gas when the [Kapuni gas field](/wiki/Kapuni "Kapuni") entered production in 1970 and a 340 km long high-pressure pipeline from the field in Taranaki to the city was completed. Auckland was connected to the [Maui gas field](/wiki/Maui_gas_field "Maui gas field") in 1982 following the completion of a high-pressure pipeline from the Maui gas pipeline near [Huntly](/wiki/Huntly,_New_Zealand "Huntly, New Zealand"), via the city, to Whangārei in Northland.[[168]](#cite_note-169)

The high-pressure transmission pipelines supplying the city are now owned and operated by [First Gas](/wiki/First_Gas "First Gas"), with [Vector](/wiki/Vector_Limited "Vector Limited") owning and operating the medium and low-pressure distribution pipelines in the city.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auckland&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Sport")]

[Rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union"), [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket"), [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league"), [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") (soccer) and [netball](/wiki/Netball "Netball") are widely played and followed.

There are three [racecourses](/wiki/Racecourses "Racecourses") within the city ([Ellerslie](/wiki/Ellerslie_Racecourse "Ellerslie Racecourse") and Avondale for thoroughbred racing, and [Alexandra Park](/wiki/Alexandra_Park,_Auckland "Alexandra Park, Auckland") for [harness racing](/wiki/Harness_racing "Harness racing")). A fourth racecourse is located at [Pukekohe](/wiki/Pukekohe_Park_Raceway "Pukekohe Park Raceway"), straddling the boundary between Auckland and the neighbouring [Waikato](/wiki/Waikato "Waikato") region. [Greyhound racing](/wiki/Greyhound_racing "Greyhound racing") is held at Manukau Stadium.

Major sporting venues:

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Eden_Park_2012.jpg/250px-Eden_Park_2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eden_Park_2012.jpg)

[Eden Park](/wiki/Eden_Park "Eden Park"), the largest stadium in Auckland by capacity with 50,000 seats

* [Eden Park](/wiki/Eden_Park "Eden Park") is the city's primary stadium and a frequent home for international rugby union and cricket matches, in addition to [Super Rugby](/wiki/Super_Rugby "Super Rugby") matches where the [Blues](/wiki/Blues_(Super_Rugby) "Blues (Super Rugby)") play their home games. It is also the home ground of [Auckland](/wiki/Auckland_Rugby_Football_Union "Auckland Rugby Football Union") in the [Mitre 10 Cup](/wiki/Mitre_10_Cup "Mitre 10 Cup"), and [Auckland](/wiki/Auckland_Aces "Auckland Aces") in domestic cricket.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Mt_SS.jpg/250px-Mt_SS.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mt_SS.jpg)

[Mount Smart Stadium](/wiki/Mount_Smart_Stadium "Mount Smart Stadium")

* [Mt Smart Stadium](/wiki/Mt_Smart_Stadium "Mt Smart Stadium") is used mainly for rugby league and association football matches and is home to the [New Zealand Warriors](/wiki/New_Zealand_Warriors "New Zealand Warriors") of the [NRL](/wiki/NRL "NRL") and [Auckland FC](/wiki/Auckland_FC "Auckland FC") of the [A-League](/wiki/A-League_Men "A-League Men"), and is also used for concerts, previously hosting the Auckland show of the annual [Big Day Out](/wiki/Big_Day_Out "Big Day Out") music festival and the [1990 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1990_Commonwealth_Games "1990 Commonwealth Games").
* [North Harbour Stadium](/wiki/North_Harbour_Stadium "North Harbour Stadium") is mainly used for rugby union and football (soccer) matches, but is also used for concerts. It is the home ground for [North Harbour](/wiki/North_Harbour_Rugby_Union "North Harbour Rugby Union") in the [Mitre 10 Cup](/wiki/Bunnings_NPC "Bunnings NPC").
* [ASB Tennis Centre](/wiki/ASB_Tennis_Centre "ASB Tennis Centre") is Auckland's primary tennis venue, hosting international tournaments for men and women ([ASB Classic](/wiki/WTA_Auckland_Open "WTA Auckland Open")) in January each year. ASB Bank took over the sponsorship of the men's tournament from 2016, the event formerly being known as the [Heineken Open](/wiki/Heineken_Open_(tennis) "Heineken Open (tennis)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Spark_Arena_20220220_131032.jpg/250px-Spark_Arena_20220220_131032.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spark_Arena_20220220_131032.jpg)

[Spark Arena](/wiki/Spark_Arena "Spark Arena")

* [Spark Arena](/wiki/Spark_Arena "Spark Arena"), previously known as Vector Arena, is an indoor auditorium primarily used for concerts and is the home of the [New Zealand Breakers](/wiki/New_Zealand_Breakers "New Zealand Breakers") basketball team. It also hosts international netball.
* [Trusts Arena](/wiki/Trusts_Arena "Trusts Arena") is an indoor venue which primarily hosts netball matches, and is the home of the [Northern Mystics](/wiki/Northern_Mystics "Northern Mystics") of the [ANZ Premiership](/wiki/ANZ_Premiership "ANZ Premiership"). It is also where the [2007 World Netball Championships](/wiki/2007_World_Netball_Championships "2007 World Netball Championships") were held. Since 2015, an annual event on the [World Series of Darts](/wiki/World_Series_of_Darts_(tour) "World Series of Darts (tour)") has been held there.
* [North Shore Events Centre](/wiki/North_Shore_Events_Centre "North Shore Events Centre") is an indoor arena which is used for a variety of sporting events, as well as concerts and expos. It was formerly home to the [New Zealand Breakers](/wiki/New_Zealand_Breakers "New Zealand Breakers") and hosted much of the [2009 FIBA Under-19 World Championship](/wiki/2009_FIBA_Under-19_World_Championship "2009 FIBA Under-19 World Championship").
* [Vodafone Events Centre](/wiki/Vodafone_Events_Centre "Vodafone Events Centre") is an indoor arena which hosts a variety of events, and is the home of the [Northern Stars](/wiki/Northern_Stars "Northern Stars") netball team of the [ANZ Premiership](/wiki/ANZ_Premiership "ANZ Premiership").
* [Pukekohe Park Raceway](/wiki/Pukekohe_Park_Raceway "Pukekohe Park Raceway") is a thoroughbred horse-racing venue that used to host a leg of the [V8 Supercars](/wiki/V8_Supercars "V8 Supercars") series annually, along with other motorsports events. The most important horse-racing meeting is held annually at the end of November, featuring the Group 2 Counties Cup and three other stakes races.
* [Western Springs Stadium](/wiki/Western_Springs_Stadium "Western Springs Stadium") has since 1929 hosted [speedway racing](/wiki/Dirt_track_racing_in_New_Zealand "Dirt track racing in New Zealand") during the summer. It also hosts concerts, with many of New Zealand's largest-ever concerts having taken place at the stadium. It is also the home of [Ponsonby RFC](/wiki/Ponsonby_RFC "Ponsonby RFC").

Major teams based in Auckland who compete in national or transnational competitions are:

* Formerly Auckland Blues, the [Blues](/wiki/Blues_(Super_Rugby) "Blues (Super Rugby)") compete in [Super Rugby](/wiki/Super_Rugby "Super Rugby"). Auckland is also home to three [Mitre 10 Cup](/wiki/Mitre_10_Cup "Mitre 10 Cup") rugby union teams: [Auckland](/wiki/Auckland_Rugby_Football_Union "Auckland Rugby Football Union"), [North Harbour](/wiki/North_Harbour_Rugby_Union "North Harbour Rugby Union") and [Counties Manukau](/wiki/Counties_Manukau_Rugby_Union "Counties Manukau Rugby Union").
* Previously Auckland Warriors, the [New Zealand Warriors](/wiki/New_Zealand_Warriors "New Zealand Warriors") are a team in Australia's [National Rugby League](/wiki/National_Rugby_League "National Rugby League") competition. They play their home games at Mt Smart Stadium. The [Akarana Falcons](/wiki/Akarana_Falcons "Akarana Falcons") and [Counties Manukau](/wiki/Counties_Manukau_rugby_league_team "Counties Manukau rugby league team") compete in the [National Competition](/wiki/NZRL_Men%27s_National_Competition "NZRL Men's National Competition").
* Auckland's men's first class cricket team, the [Auckland Aces](/wiki/Auckland_Aces "Auckland Aces"), play their home matches at Eden Park, generally on the outer oval. The women's team, the [Auckland Hearts](/wiki/Auckland_Hearts "Auckland Hearts"), play at Melville Park in [Epsom](/wiki/Epsom,_New_Zealand "Epsom, New Zealand").
* [Auckland FC](/wiki/Auckland_FC "Auckland FC") are a professional football club that compete in the [A-League Men](/wiki/A-League_Men "A-League Men") competition, and will join [A-League Women](/wiki/A-League_Women "A-League Women") in the 2027/28 season. The football club play their home games at Mt Smart Stadium
* [Auckland City](/wiki/Auckland_City_FC "Auckland City FC"), [Auckland United](/wiki/Auckland_United_FC "Auckland United FC"), and [Eastern Suburbs](/wiki/Eastern_Suburbs_AFC "Eastern Suburbs AFC") are football teams play in the [Northern League](/wiki/Northern_League_(New_Zealand) "Northern League (New Zealand)").
* [Northern Mystics](/wiki/Northern_Mystics "Northern Mystics") and [Northern Stars](/wiki/Northern_Stars "Northern Stars") are netball teams who compete in the [ANZ Premiership](/wiki/ANZ_Premiership "ANZ Premiership"). The Mystics play their home games at [Trusts Stadium](/wiki/Trusts_Stadium "Trusts Stadium") and the Stars at the [Vodafone Events Centre](/wiki/Vodafone_Events_Centre "Vodafone Events Centre").
* [New Zealand Breakers](/wiki/New_Zealand_Breakers "New Zealand Breakers") are a basketball team who compete in the [Australian National Basketball League](/wiki/Australian_National_Basketball_League "Australian National Basketball League") and play their home games primarily at Spark Arena. The [Auckland Tuatara](/wiki/Auckland_Tuatara_(basketball) "Auckland Tuatara (basketball)") and [Franklin Bulls](/wiki/Franklin_Bulls "Franklin Bulls") play in the [New Zealand National Basketball League](/wiki/New_Zealand_National_Basketball_League "New Zealand National Basketball League").
* [Botany Swarm](/wiki/Botany_Swarm "Botany Swarm") and [West Auckland Admirals](/wiki/West_Auckland_Admirals "West Auckland Admirals") compete in the [New Zealand Ice Hockey League](/wiki/New_Zealand_Ice_Hockey_League "New Zealand Ice Hockey League").
* [Auckland Tuatara](/wiki/Auckland_Tuatara_(baseball) "Auckland Tuatara (baseball)") had previously competed in the [Australian Baseball League](/wiki/Australian_Baseball_League "Australian Baseball League").

Annual sporting events include:

* The [ATP Auckland Open](/wiki/ATP_Auckland_Open "ATP Auckland Open") and the [WTA Auckland Open](/wiki/WTA_Auckland_Open "WTA Auckland Open") (both known for sponsorship reasons as the ASB Classic), are men's and women's tennis tournaments, respectively, which are held annually at the [ASB Tennis Centre](/wiki/ASB_Tennis_Centre "ASB Tennis Centre") in January. The men's tournament has been held since 1956, and the women's tournament since 1986.
* The [Auckland Super400](/wiki/Auckland_Super400 "Auckland Super400") (known for sponsorship reasons as the ITM Auckland Super 400) was a [Supercars Championship](/wiki/Supercars_Championship "Supercars Championship") race held at [Pukekohe Park Raceway](/wiki/Pukekohe_Park_Raceway "Pukekohe Park Raceway"). The race has been held intermittently since 1996
* The [Auckland Marathon](/wiki/Auckland_Marathon "Auckland Marathon") (and half-marathon) is an annual marathon It is the largest marathon in New Zealand and draws in the vicinity of 15,000 entrants. It has been held annually since 1992.
* The [Auckland Anniversary Regatta](/wiki/Auckland_Anniversary_Regatta "Auckland Anniversary Regatta") is a sailing regatta which has been held annually since 1840, the year of Auckland's founding. It is held over [Auckland Anniversary](/wiki/Auckland_Anniversary "Auckland Anniversary") weekend and attracts several hundred entrants each year. It is the largest such regatta, and the oldest sporting event, in New Zealand.
* [Auckland Cup Week](/wiki/Auckland_Cup_Week "Auckland Cup Week") is an annual [horse racing](/wiki/Horse_racing "Horse racing") carnival, which has been held in early March since its inception in 2006. It is the richest such carnival in New Zealand, and incorporates several of New Zealand's major thoroughbred horse races, including the [Auckland Cup](/wiki/Auckland_Cup "Auckland Cup"), held since 1874, and [New Zealand Derby](/wiki/New_Zealand_Derby "New Zealand Derby"), held since 1875.
* The [Auckland Harbour Crossing Swim](/wiki/Auckland_Harbour_Crossing_Swim "Auckland Harbour Crossing Swim") is an annual summer swimming event. The swim crosses the [Waitematā Harbour](/wiki/Waitemat%C4%81_Harbour "Waitematā Harbour"), from the North Shore to the [Viaduct Basin](/wiki/Viaduct_Basin "Viaduct Basin") covering 2.8 km (often with some considerable counter-currents). The event has been held since 2004 and attracts over a thousand mostly amateur entrants each year, making it New Zealand's largest ocean swim.[[169]](#cite_note-170)
* Round the Bays is an annual [fun-run](/wiki/Road_running "Road running"). The course travels eastwards along the Auckland waterfront, with the run starting in the [CBD](/wiki/Auckland_CBD "Auckland CBD") and ending in [St Heliers](/wiki/Saint_Heliers "Saint Heliers"), the total length being 8.4 km (5.2 mi). It is the largest fun-run in New Zealand and attracts tens of thousands of entrants each year, with the number of entrants reported to have peaked at 80,000 in 1982. It has been held annually since [1972](/wiki/Timeline_of_New_Zealand_history#1970s "Timeline of New Zealand history").[[170]](#cite_note-171)

Major events previously held in Auckland include the [1950 British Empire Games](/wiki/1950_British_Empire_Games "1950 British Empire Games") and the [Commonwealth Games in 1990](/wiki/1990_Commonwealth_Games "1990 Commonwealth Games"),[[171]](#cite_note-DOING-172) and a number of matches (including the semi-finals and the final) of the [1987 Rugby World Cup](/wiki/1987_Rugby_World_Cup "1987 Rugby World Cup") and [2011 Rugby World Cup](/wiki/2011_Rugby_World_Cup "2011 Rugby World Cup").[[172]](#cite_note-173) Auckland hosted the [America's Cup](/wiki/America%27s_Cup "America's Cup") and [Louis Vuitton Cup](/wiki/Louis_Vuitton_Cup "Louis Vuitton Cup") in 2000, 2003, and 2021. The [2007 World Netball Championships](/wiki/2007_World_Netball_Championships "2007 World Netball Championships") were held at the Trusts Stadium. The [ITU World Triathlon Series](/wiki/ITU_World_Triathlon_Series "ITU World Triathlon Series") held a Grand Final event in the [Auckland CBD](/wiki/Auckland_CBD "Auckland CBD") from 2012 until 2015.[[173]](#cite_note-174) The [NRL Auckland Nines](/wiki/NRL_Auckland_Nines "NRL Auckland Nines") was a [rugby league nines](/wiki/Rugby_league_nines "Rugby league nines") preseason competition played at Eden Park from 2014 to 2017. The 2017 [World Masters Games](/wiki/World_Masters_Games "World Masters Games") were held at a number of venues around Auckland.[[174]](#cite_note-175) The Auckland Darts Masters was held annually at [The Trusts Arena](/wiki/The_Trusts_Arena "The Trusts Arena") from 2015 to 2018.

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Auckland&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Education")]

See also: [List of schools in the Auckland Region](/wiki/List_of_schools_in_the_Auckland_Region "List of schools in the Auckland Region")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/University_of_Auckland_Clock_Tower.jpg/250px-University_of_Auckland_Clock_Tower.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_of_Auckland_Clock_Tower.jpg)

The [University of Auckland](/wiki/University_of_Auckland "University of Auckland") clock tower building is a 'Category I' historic place, completed in 1926.[[175]](#cite_note-176)

The Auckland urban area has 340 primary schools, 80 secondary schools, and 29 composite (primary/secondary combined) schools as of February 2012, catering for roughly 250,000 students. The majority are state schools, but 63 schools are state-integrated and 39 are private.[[176]](#cite_note-177)

The city is home to some of the largest schools in terms of students in New Zealand, including [Mt Albert Grammar School](/wiki/Mount_Albert_Grammar_School "Mount Albert Grammar School"), the second-largest school in New Zealand with a student population of 3035,[[177]](#cite_note-178) and [Rangitoto College](/wiki/Rangitoto_College "Rangitoto College") in the East Coast Bays area, the largest school in New Zealand with 4,071 students as of October 2025.[[178]](#cite_note-NZ_school_roll_data-179)

Auckland has some of the largest [universities in the country](/wiki/Tertiary_education_in_New_Zealand "Tertiary education in New Zealand"). Five of New Zealand's eight universities and eight of its fifteen polytechnics have campuses in Auckland. The [University of Auckland](/wiki/University_of_Auckland "University of Auckland"), [Auckland University of Technology](/wiki/Auckland_University_of_Technology "Auckland University of Technology"), [Manukau Institute of Technology](/wiki/Manukau_Institute_of_Technology "Manukau Institute of Technology"), and [Unitec Institute of Technology](/wiki/Unitec_Institute_of_Technology "Unitec Institute of Technology") are based in Auckland. Of institutions based in other regions, the [University of Otago](/wiki/University_of_Otago "University of Otago"), [Victoria University of Wellington](/wiki/Victoria_University_of_Wellington "Victoria University of Wellington"), [Massey University](/wiki/Massey_University "Massey University") and several polytechnics have satellite campuses in Auckland.[[179]](#cite_note-180)

Auckland is a major centre of overseas language education, with large numbers of foreign students (particularly East Asians) coming to the city for several months or years to learn English or study at universities – although numbers New Zealand-wide have dropped substantially since peaking in 2003.[[180]](#cite_note-181) As of 2007[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Auckland&action=edit), there are around 50 [New Zealand Qualifications Authority](/wiki/New_Zealand_Qualifications_Authority "New Zealand Qualifications Authority") (NZQA) certified schools and institutes teaching English in the Auckland area.[[181]](#cite_note-182)
